title: Photon Anomalous Blockade in Waveguide Cavity QED with Atomic Mirrors
authors: Yang Xue, Yue Chang, Tao Shi, Yu-xi Liu
abstract: Waveguide cavity quantum electrodynamics (QED) with atomic mirrors is a growing research area of quantum optics and can be applied to quantum information processing. We here study the photon statistics of output fields from a waveguide cavity QED system, in which the waveguide is coupled to quantized mirror atoms and one driven medium atom. Our results show that the photon blockade can occur even for a bad atom cavity with large dissipation and small coupling between the medium atom and the cavity, in contrast to the small dissipation and the strong coupling of the medium atom to the cavity field for the conventional photon blockade or the quantum interference for the unconventional photon blockade in the cavity QED system. Utilizing both the master equation and scattering theories, we derive the condition under which the photon blockade occurs in weakly driven systems. We find that such photon anomalous blockade is due to the quantum Zeno effect and is robust against variations of the medium atom's position within the cavity. Our study paves a way to exploit the photon blockade and single-photon devices via the waveguide cavity QED.
summary: This paper identifies a new type of photon blockade in waveguide cavity QED that works even in high-loss, weak-coupling environments. The effect is driven by the Quantum Zeno effect rather than traditional strong coupling. This discovery offers a potential pathway for creating robust single-photon devices using existing waveguide QED platforms.
main_problem: The study investigates the emergence of a photon anomalous blockade in a waveguide cavity QED system, specifically in the 'bad-cavity' regime where conventional blockade mechanisms are absent.
main_result: The authors discover that photon blockade can occur in a high-dissipation, weak-coupling regime due to the Quantum Zeno effect, and that this effect is robust against the position of the medium atom and can be enhanced by using N-atom mirrors.
method: The researchers utilize both master equation and scattering theories, employing a non-Hermitian effective Hamiltonian and analyzing the system within single- and two-excitation subspaces.
model_or_system: A waveguide cavity QED system consisting of a medium atom coupled to two quantized mirror atoms (or N-atom mirrors) within a waveguide, modeled using a Lindblad master equation and a non-Hermitian Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Second-order correlation functions g(2)(0) and g(2)(tau), photon statistics, and the energy spectrum (eigenvalues).
important_parameters: Decay rates of mirror atoms (Gamma) and medium atom (gamma), detuning (Delta), driving strength (epsilon), and the number of atoms in the mirror (N).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a weakly driven probe field and applies the Markovian approximation.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the schematic of the waveguide cavity system and a comparison to a Fabry-Perot cavity; Fig 2 illustrates the transition diagram and the energy spectrum of the system.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the waveguide cavity QED setup, develops the theoretical framework using master equations and effective Hamiltonians, analyzes the single- and two-excitation subspaces to identify the blockade mechanism, explores scaling with N-atom mirrors, and discusses experimental feasibility.
why_interesting: It provides a novel mechanism (Quantum Zeno effect) for photon blockade in dissipative regimes, which is highly relevant for developing robust single-photon sources in open quantum systems and waveguide-based quantum networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematics for the waveguide cavity QED system in which two quantized atom mirrors (brown) and a medium atom (blue) are coupled to the waveguide. The medium atom located at xp = 0 is driven by a coherent light. The mirror and medium atoms are three identical two-level systems with the frequency ω0 corresponding to a wavelength λ0 of pho- tons. The waveguide cavity with the quantized atom mirrors is similar to the Fabry-Perot cavity with two parallel classical mirrors as schematically shown in (b).

FIG. 2. (a) The transition diagram of the system when a probe field is applied to the medium atom. Each red line with two arrows denotes the probe field induced transitions between states. Each blue line with single-arrow means the decay. Each green curve with two arrows means the waveg- uide induced mixing of two connected states. The coupling strength is J = √2Γγ. Here, the states |ep, D⟩= |ep⟩⊗|D⟩ and |gp, D⟩= |gp⟩⊗|D⟩include the branch of the dark state |D⟩, the states |ep, B⟩= |ep⟩⊗|B⟩and |gp, B⟩= |gp⟩⊗|B⟩in- clude the branch of the bright state |B⟩. (b) Energy spectrum of the system for different decay rates γ of the medium atom. The lower energy splitting (solid line) represents the...

FIG. 3. (a) and (b) Correlation functions g(2)(0) as a function of the detuning ∆/√2Γγ for different driving strengths ϵ. The decay rate of the medium atom is γ = 0.01Γ in (a) and γ = Γ in (b). (c) Correlation functions g(2)(0) obtained by the ana- lytical results (blue solid, green dashed and red dotted curves) and direct numerical calculations by solving master equation (blue points, green squares, and red triangles) for different medium-atom decay rates γ. (d) Correlation functions g(2)(τ) as a function of time τ for distances d = |xp −x1| = λ0/4 and d = λ0/10, with separation between two mirror atoms fixed at |x2 −x1| = λ0/2 and detuning ∆= Re(λ−) ≈√2Γγ.
